perturbation.original_text: The obligations of Scientific Games and MDI under this paragraph (4) shall be subject to their disclosure obligations under federal and state securities laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party understands and acknowledges that the other party is making no representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Material, and neither the disclosing party, nor any of its respective officers, directors, employees, stockholders, affiliates or agents will have any liability to the other party or any other person resulting from such other party's use of the Evaluation Material. The obligations of Scientific Games and MDI under this paragraph (4) shall be subject to their disclosure obligations under federal and state securities laws. Only those representations or warranties that are made to MDI or Scientific Games, as the case may be, in a Definitive Agreement when, as, and if it is executed, and subject to such limitations and restrictions as may be specified in such Definitive Agreement, will have any legal effect.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the disclosure about obligations under federal and state securities laws into a section about accuracy and completeness of evaluation material makes it less prominent and harder to find, potentially hindering compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Securities Exchange Act of 1934 requires public companies to make full and fair disclosures of all material information to investors. By burying a disclosure obligation within a clause concerning the accuracy of evaluation materials, the modified text reduces the visibility of obligations under the Securities Exchange Act, potentially undermining investor protection.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 78j - Manipulative and deceptive devices | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 78j - Manipulative and deceptive devices U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next It shall be unlawful for any person , directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange— (a) (1) To effect a short sale , or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. (2) Paragraph (1) of this subsection shall not apply to security futures products . (b) To use or employ, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security registered on a national securities exchange or any security not so registered, or any securities-based swap agreement [1] any manipulative or deceptive device or contrivance in contravention of such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. (c) (1) To effect, accept, or facilitate a transaction involving the loan or borrowing of securities in contravention of such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. (2) Nothing in paragraph (1) may be construed to limit the authority of the appropriate Federal banking agency (as defined in section 1813(q) of title 12 ), the National Credit Union Administration , or any other Federal department or agency having a responsibility under Federal law to prescribe rules or regulations restricting transactions involving the loan or borrowing of securities in or
perturbation.justification: The modified text buries the disclosure obligation within a section about evaluation material, potentially reducing its visibility. This contradicts the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, which, as interpreted by the LLM, requires full and fair disclosures. The structural flaw of placing a critical disclosure in a less prominent location undermines investor protection. Snippets 1 and 2 both point to 15 U.S. Code § 78j, which prohibits 'manipulative or deceptive device or contrivance' which could be argued to include obscuring legally required disclosures.

perturbation.original_text: It is further understood and agreed that no failure or delay by either party in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder will operate as a waiver thereof, nor will any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any right, power or privilege hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party understands and acknowledges that the other party is making no representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Material, and neither the disclosing party, nor any of its respective officers, directors, employees, stockholders, affiliates or agents will have any liability to the other party or any other person resulting from such other party's use of the Evaluation Material. It is further understood and agreed that no failure or delay by either party in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder will operate as a waiver thereof, nor will any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any right, power or privilege hereunder. Only those representations or warranties that are made to MDI or Scientific Games, as the case may be, in a Definitive Agreement when, as, and if it is executed, and subject to such limitations and restrictions as may be specified in such Definitive Agreement, will have any legal effect.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'no waiver' clause to the representations and warranties section. By doing so it may cause parties to believe a failure to act may limit their legal rights, contradicting the purpose of the clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts discusses waivers. By placing the no-waiver clause amidst disclaimers about the accuracy of the evaluation material, a party may wrongly believe that certain failures to act related to the evaluation material will constitute a waiver of rights. This is contrary to the intent of the no-waiver clause, which protects against unintended waiver of rights due to inaction or delay.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: waiver | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you waiver A waiver is the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege . In legal contexts, a waiver is often documented in writing, but it can also be implied through actions . By waiving a right, an individual or party chooses not to enforce a particular right, claim , or provision they are legally entitled to. perturbation.justification: The changed text potentially contradicts the original intent of the 'no waiver' clause. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that moving the 'no waiver' clause to the representations and warranties section might cause parties to believe a failure to act could limit their legal rights. The Law Explanation supports this by mentioning that a party may wrongly believe that certain failures to act related to the evaluation material will constitute a waiver of rights. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines a waiver as 'the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege' and indicates that it can be 'implied through actions'. Placing the no-waiver clause within the context of disclaimers about evaluation material accuracy could lead to an interpretation where inaction is construed as a waiver, thereby undermining the protection the clause originally intended to provide against unintentional waivers.
